1.matchText: regional cultures of the previous chapter
1.blurb: The collapse of the Moche, Nazca, Lima, and Recuay cultures across the sixth century CE — driven by El Niño floods, drought, deforestation, and political fragmentation — is the closing chapter of the Andean Kingdoms TL. The Middle Horizon empires step into the vacuum that chapter describes, building highland states on the wreckage of the coastal regional cultures.

The vertical geography of the Andes — coast, highland, puna, eastern jungle, all stacked within a few miles horizontally — has shaped Andean civilization since the first sedentary villages of the Late Preceramic. The Early Andean Civilizations narrative opens with a chapter on this geography, and every later Andean culture (Chavín, Moche, Tiwanaku, Wari, Inca) operated within the same vertical frame.

While Tiwanaku and Wari were forming as imperial states in the Andes, the Maya Classic period was at its peak in the Yucatán — Tikal and Calakmul fighting their long wars, royal dynasties commissioning the inscribed stone stelae that document Maya political history in detail. The Maya were writing dynastic histories on stone while the Wari were not writing anything, which is why we know the Maya political narrative and have to reconstruct the Wari one from architecture.

Teotihuacan in the Valley of Mexico was just past its peak in the seventh century CE and beginning the slow decline that would empty the city by 750 CE. The Mesoamerican imperial peer of the Middle Horizon Andean states, it ran a comparable urban-administrative project on the same continent at the same time — and like Tiwanaku and Wari, it left no decipherable writing about itself.

2.blurb: Lake Titicaca, the highest navigable lake in the world at 12,500 feet, was already supporting settled human populations by 1500 BCE — millennia before Tiwanaku rose. The Early Andean Civilizations TL covers the deep prehistory of the altiplano basin and the development of the camelid herding and tuber agriculture that made highland civilization possible.

The Staff God image first appeared at Chavín de Huántar around 500 BCE on the Raimondi Stele — a frontal figure holding paired staffs, with a fanged face and elaborate radiating headdress. Chavín established the iconographic template that Tiwanaku would revive a thousand years later, and the same template would propagate through Wari to the Inca creator god Wiracocha. Two thousand years of unbroken iconographic transmission, with Chavín as the source.

Chavín de Huántar in the northern Peruvian highlands was the great religious pilgrimage center of the Early Horizon, between roughly 900 and 200 BCE. Its underground galleries, the Lanzón monolith, and the Raimondi Stele established the religious vocabulary — the Staff God, the kenning art style, the use of psychoactive plants in ritual — that the Middle Horizon empires would inherit a thousand years later.

3.blurb: Pikillaqta in the Cusco basin, the most spectacular Wari planned administrative city, sits in territory that the Andean Kingdoms TL covers as the southern frontier of the Recuay highland tradition. The transition from regional Recuay-style chieftaincies to imposed Wari grid-city administration is one of the most visible contrasts in central Peruvian highland archaeology — local organic settlement giving way to imperial rectangular planning.

The Tang capital at Chang'an (modern Xi'an) was the largest planned city in the world in the seventh century — a rigorous rectangular grid of over 100 city blocks within massive walls, designed to project Chinese imperial cosmology in physical form. Pikillaqta would fit inside one of Chang'an's neighborhood blocks, but the architectural concept is recognizably similar: imperial central planning expressing state authority through rigorous geometric layout.

The 2012 discovery of the Castillo de Huarmey royal mausoleum on the north-central coast extended back into Wari territory the same warrior-priest burial pattern documented in the Andean Kingdoms TL for the Moche elite — Lord of Sipán, Lady of Cao, the priestess of San José de Moro. The Wari elite at Huarmey were buried in the same regional tradition, with similar quantities of gold and craft offerings, but with the addition of distinctive Wari iconography on the textiles.

4.blurb: The Moquegua Valley where Cerro Baúl sits is one of the few places in the central Andes where coastal, mid-elevation, and high-elevation crops can all be grown within a day's walk. The Andean Kingdoms TL covers the regional cultures that worked this valley before Wari and Tiwanaku arrived — the same vertical-archipelago logic that made Moquegua attractive to the Middle Horizon empires made it attractive to the regional cultures before them.

Julio C. Tello, the Quechua-born Peruvian archaeologist who became the founding figure of indigenous Andean archaeology, did the foundational work on the Paracas necropolis in the 1920s and went on to distinguish Wari from Tiwanaku as separate empires in the 1930s. Before Tello, the term 'Tiahuanaco' was used indiscriminately for both Middle Horizon empires; after Tello, the central Andean record could be read as the work of two distinct imperial traditions.

The Tang Dynasty maintained an active diplomatic and tributary network across East and Central Asia — embassies to and from Korea, Japan, the Tibetan Empire, the Uyghur Khaganate, the Abbasid caliphate after 750 — all documented in detailed surviving Tang records. The contrast with the Andean record is total: the Tang kept letters, treaties, and embassy logs, while Tiwanaku and Wari left no comparable diplomatic documentation despite running comparable interstate relations.

5.blurb: Chicha — the lightly alcoholic corn beer that lubricated Andean political relationships — was already central to the Lima and Recuay cultures of the Regional Development Period. The Andean Kingdoms TL covers chicha production in the central coast and northern highlands, where the same reciprocal-feasting protocol that Cerro Baúl institutionalized for diplomatic relations was being developed at smaller community scale.

5.matchText: Andean political authority was constituted through reciprocal feasting
5.blurb: The reciprocal-feasting protocol that constituted Andean political authority has a Mesoamerican analogue in the Aztec institution of the macehualtin tribute feast — large-scale state-hosted banquets that bound the Triple Alliance's tributary populations into formal subordination through accepted hospitality. Both systems used the social ambiguity of the feast to perform political hierarchy without explicit subordination language.

The systematic excavation of Cerro Baúl by Patrick Williams and Donna Nash since the late 1980s has done for the Wari-Tiwanaku interaction what the earlier Sipán and El Brujo excavations did for Moche elite burials — providing a single richly preserved site that anchors a much larger interpretive framework for the surrounding period. The Cerro Baúl find is the southern-frontier counterpart to the great north-coast Moche tombs covered in the Andean Kingdoms TL.

6.blurb: The Inca road system that the Spanish marveled at in the sixteenth century was, in its central Peruvian core, largely a refurbishment of the older Wari network. The Andean Kingdoms TL covers the predecessor coastal road systems — the Moche valley roads and the Nazca ceremonial pathways — that fed into the broader Andean engineering tradition Wari then synthesized into the first integrated imperial road network.

The Tang Dynasty was simultaneously spreading Mandarin Chinese as the administrative language of the largest empire in the world — through bureaucratic imposition, literary patronage, and the imperial examination system. The Tang and Wari linguistic projects were running in parallel: state expansion carrying a particular dialect into provincial use, with the dialect outlasting the empire that spread it. Mandarin and Quechua both owe their modern geographic reach to seventh-century imperial administration.

The Roman road network at its peak covered roughly 50,000 miles across Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East — the gold standard of pre-modern imperial infrastructure that all later road comparisons measure against. The Wari road network was an order of magnitude smaller, but operating on the same principle: paved engineered routes built and maintained by the central state to project administrative reach across imperial geography.

7.blurb: The Staff God carved on the Chavín Raimondi Stele around 500 BCE established the visual template — frontal pose, paired staffs, fanged face, radiating headdress — that Tiwanaku revived, Wari adopted, and the Inca preserved as the central image of their creator god Wiracocha. Two thousand years of unbroken iconographic transmission across multiple successor civilizations is one of the deepest features of Andean religion.

The Eleusinian Mysteries of ancient Greece almost certainly involved a psychoactive beverage (probably containing ergot alkaloids derived from infected barley) consumed by initiates as part of the initiation ritual. The institutional embedding of visionary plant use in religion is unusual but not unique to the Andes — Greek, Vedic, and Mesoamerican traditions all developed parallel forms, though the Andean version was more systematically integrated into elite religious practice.

Soma, the mysterious psychoactive plant or fungus consumed by Brahmin priests in early Vedic ritual, is mentioned obsessively in the Sanskrit hymns of the Rig Veda. The Vedic and Andean cases together suggest that systematic ritual use of psychoactive plants by religious specialists was a recurring solution to the problem of how to authorize religious authority — the priest who has met the god in vision is the priest qualified to mediate with the god on the community's behalf.

8.blurb: The Late Intermediate Period is the four-century gap between the Middle Horizon collapse and the Inca conquest — a period of regional fragmentation that produced the Chimú, Chancay, Aymara kingdoms, and the Killke chieftaincies of the Cusco basin. It mirrors structurally the Early Intermediate Period (Andean Kingdoms) that came between Chavín and the Middle Horizon: imperial collapse → regional fragmentation → next imperial cycle. The Andes ran this cycle three times across two thousand years.

The Maya Classic collapse — a cascade of city abandonments across the southern Yucatán between roughly 800 and 1000 CE — happened almost simultaneously with the Tiwanaku-Wari collapse and almost certainly had related climatic causation. The Maya, Tang, and Andean collapses together make the late first millennium one of the great moments of climate-driven imperial demolition in world history.

The Tang Dynasty collapsed in 907 CE, followed by the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period of fragmentation — the Chinese version of the same late-first-millennium imperial demolition that took down Tiwanaku, Wari, and the Maya Classic centers within the same century. Three different continents, three different imperial traditions, and at least partly shared climatic causation.

The Inca and the Aztec were the two great late pre-Columbian empires the Spanish encountered in the early sixteenth century — the Inca in 1532, the Aztec in 1519. Both were built on the institutional inheritance of older imperial traditions (Wari and Tiwanaku for the Inca; Toltec and Teotihuacan for the Aztec) and both fell in less than a century to small Spanish forces equipped with steel, gunpowder, horses, and Old World diseases. The structural parallel is striking; the conquests were nearly synchronous.
